event: Neolithic farming communities settle in Thessaly and other regions, marking the beginning of agriculture in Greece.
The Cycladic civilization emerges in the Aegean islands, producing distinctive marble figurines.
The Minoan civilization begins to develop on Crete, becoming Europe's first advanced civilization.
Minoan palace complexes such as Knossos are built, centering a sophisticated maritime trading culture.
The Mycenaean civilization rises on the Greek mainland, building fortified citadels like Mycenae and Tiryns.
Mycenaean Greeks come to dominate Crete as Minoan power declines following volcanic and other disruptions.
The Mycenaean palace centers collapse, beginning the Greek Dark Ages.
The first recorded ancient Olympic Games are held at Olympia.
Greek city-states (poleis) form and the Greek alphabet develops, while Homer's epics take shape.
Solon introduces sweeping legal and economic reforms in Athens, laying foundations for democracy.
Cleisthenes establishes democratic institutions in Athens, creating the world's first democracy.
Athenians defeat the invading Persians at the Battle of Marathon.
Greek forces hold at Thermopylae and win the decisive naval Battle of Salamis against Persia.
Construction of the Parthenon begins on the Acropolis under Pericles during Athens' Golden Age.
The Peloponnesian War erupts between Athens and Sparta and their allies.
Athens surrenders to Sparta, ending the Peloponnesian War and Athenian hegemony.
Philip II of Macedon defeats the Greek city-states at Chaeronea, uniting Greece under Macedonian control.
Alexander the Great becomes king of Macedon and begins conquests spreading Greek culture across Asia.
Alexander the Great dies in Babylon, ushering in the Hellenistic age of successor kingdoms.
Rome conquers Greece after the sack of Corinth, incorporating it into the Roman Republic.
Emperor Constantine refounds Byzantium as Constantinople, capital of the eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire.
The Great Schism splits Eastern Orthodox Christianity from the Roman Catholic Church.
The Fourth Crusade sacks Constantinople and fragments Byzantine Greece among Latin states.
The Ottoman Turks capture Constantinople, ending the Byzantine Empire.
Athens falls to the Ottomans, completing Ottoman rule over most of the Greek lands.
A Venetian bombardment severely damages the Parthenon during the Morean War.
The Greek War of Independence begins against Ottoman rule.
Allied British, French, and Russian fleets destroy the Ottoman-Egyptian navy at the Battle of Navarino.
The London Protocol recognizes Greece as an independent sovereign state.
Otto of Bavaria is installed as the first king of the new Kingdom of Greece.
Britain cedes the Ionian Islands to Greece, expanding the kingdom.
Athens hosts the first modern Olympic Games.
Greece joins the First Balkan War, gaining Thessaloniki and large territories from the Ottomans.
The Treaty of Bucharest after the Balkan Wars greatly enlarges Greek territory, including Crete and Macedonia.
Greece launches a military campaign into Anatolia following World War I.
The Asia Minor Catastrophe ends with Greek defeat and the loss of Smyrna.
The Treaty of Lausanne mandates a massive population exchange between Greece and Turkey.
Greece is proclaimed a republic after a referendum abolishes the monarchy.
Greece repels the Italian invasion, marking an early Allied victory in World War II.
Nazi Germany invades and occupies Greece alongside Italian and Bulgarian forces.
Greece is liberated from Axis occupation as German forces withdraw.
The Greek Civil War breaks out between government and communist forces.
The Greek Civil War ends with the defeat of the communist insurgency.
Greece joins NATO, aligning firmly with the Western bloc.
A military junta seizes power in a coup, establishing the Regime of the Colonels.
The military dictatorship collapses and democracy is restored under Konstantinos Karamanlis.
A new constitution establishes Greece as a parliamentary republic after a referendum abolishes the monarchy.
Greece becomes a member of the European Economic Community, forerunner of the European Union.
Greece adopts the euro, joining the eurozone.
Athens hosts the Summer Olympic Games, and Greece wins the UEFA European football championship.
Greece enters a severe debt crisis and receives the first international bailout amid austerity measures.
Greek voters reject bailout terms in a referendum, but the government accepts a third rescue package.
Greece exits its third bailout program, ending years of international financial supervision.
